zigzag.definition: The zigzag $Z_L$ is a vacuum graph and $\phi^4$ primitive with $L$ loops and $2L$ propagators, formed by connecting vertices $1, \ldots, L{+}1$ with nearest-neighbour and next-to-nearest-neighbour edges plus the closing edge $\{1, L{+}1\}$. Its period is a rational multiple of $\zeta(2L{-}3)$ at every loop order. The smallest member $Z_3 = K_4$ coincides with the three-loop wheel $W_3$; note that parts of the older self-energy literature shift the loop count by one relative to the convention used here.

definingIntegral.note: Projective $\phi^4$ period (a VACUUM-period integral): $\Psi_G$ is the first Symanzik (Kirchhoff) graph polynomial of the zigzag $Z_n$ ($2n$ edges, $n$ loops), $\Omega_{2n-1}$ the projective volume form on the simplex $\Delta\subset\mathbb{P}^{2n-1}$. Transcribed from Brown-Schnetz arXiv:1208.1890 Eq. (1.1) (Brown-Schnetz period convention).

closedForm.wl:
(* Zigzag period: P_{Z_L} *)
PZigzag[L_Integer] := 4 (2 L - 2)!/(L! (L - 1)!) (1 - (1 - (-1)^L)/2^(2 L - 3)) Zeta[2 L - 3]
closedForm.normalization: $\phi^4$ projective period at $D = 4$, normalization factor $1$. This is the period of the graph, not a momentum-space integral.

zigzag.normalization: $\phi^4$ projective period at $D=4$; normalization factor $1$; NOT comparable to dim-reg momentum-space vacuum integrals (those are scaleless/divergent)

refs.description: Proves the zigzag conjecture: the period of the zigzag graph $Z_n$ is $(2n{-}3)!/(n!(n{-}1)!)$ times $4(1-(1-(-1)^n)/2^{2n-3})\,\zeta(2n{-}3)$, establishing the only infinite family of $\phi^4$ primitives with known amplitudes.
Conjectures the zigzag period identity by evaluating all $\phi^4$ primitive divergences to 7 loops, linking individual Feynman diagrams to knot-theoretic invariants and specific zeta values.

ladder4pt.definition: The planar $L$-rung box ladder has $L$ loops, $3L{+}1$ massless propagators, and four off-shell external legs at the corners of the ladder. Its value is the Usyukina-Davydychev function $\Phi^{(L)}(x,y)$, a weight-$2L$ single-valued polylogarithm known in closed form at all loop orders. The $L = 1$ member is the four-mass box. These graphs also appear as the scalar skeleton of the Coronado octagon correlator in $\mathcal{N} = 4$ SYM and of the Yangian-invariant massive tiling-cut deformation.

definingIntegral.note: In dual coordinates $x_i$, with $x_{ij}^2=(x_i-x_j)^2$: the $L$-rung box ladder defined iteratively from the four-mass box $I^{(1)}=\int d^4x_r/(i\pi^2\,x_{1r}^2 x_{3r}^2 x_{5r}^2 x_{7r}^2)$; its value is the Usyukina-Davydychev $\Phi^{(L)}$ (Davydychev/UD, PLB 305 (1993) 136). Iterative dual form transcribed from Caron-Huot-Dixon et al. arXiv:1806.01361 Eqs. (2.12)-(2.13).

refs.description: Introduces an approach for evaluating three- and four-point massless ladder diagrams in terms of polylogarithms, using the uniqueness method and Mellin-Barnes representations.
Derives the exact closed-form result $\Phi^{(L)}(x,y)$ for three- and four-point ladder integrals with an arbitrary number of rungs, expressing them in terms of classical polylogarithms.

Establishes identities between classes of off-shell four-point conformal integrals (including box ladders), proving dual conformal invariance that later became central to the amplitudes program in $\mathcal{N}=4$ SYM.

ladder4pt.variants: 3-pt triangle ladders share Phi^(L); seeded later as a mass-config variant, not a separate slug

wheel.definition: The Broadhurst wheel $W_L$ is a vacuum graph consisting of a central hub vertex connected by $L$ spokes to an outer $L$-cycle, giving $L$ loops and $2L$ propagators. It is a $\phi^4$ primitive whose period equals $\binom{2L{-}2}{L{-}1}\,\zeta(2L{-}3)$. The smallest member $W_3 = K_4$ coincides with the zigzag $Z_3$.

definingIntegral.note: Projective $\phi^4$ period (VACUUM-period integral): $\Psi_G$ is the first Symanzik (Kirchhoff) polynomial of the wheel $W_n$ ($2n$ edges, $n$ loops), $\Omega_{2n-1}$ the projective volume form on the simplex $\Delta\subset\mathbb{P}^{2n-1}$. Same period machinery as zigzag; convention from Brown-Schnetz arXiv:1208.1890 Eq. (1.1), wheel periods in Broadhurst arXiv:1604.08027.

closedForm.wl:
(* Wheel period: P_{W_L} *)
PWheel[L_Integer] := Binomial[2 L - 2, L - 1] Zeta[2 L - 3]
closedForm.normalization: $\phi^4$ projective period at $D = 4$, normalization factor $1$. This is the period of the graph, not a momentum-space integral.

wheel.normalization: $\phi^4$ projective period at $D=4$; normalization factor $1$; same vacuum-period integral as zigzag, NOT comparable to dim-reg momentum-space vacuum integrals

refs.description: Evaluates all 216 massless scalar diagrams through five loops using conformal invariance and recurrence relations, identifying the wheel periods as products of Riemann zeta values.
Studies graph invariants of $\phi^4$ primitives, including the wheel family, establishing that the Feynman period is preserved under planar duality, Schnetz twist, and vertex deletion.

basso-dixon.definition: The Basso-Dixon fishnet $B_{M,N}$ is an $M \times N$ rectangular grid of massless propagators with four off-shell external legs at the corners, giving $MN$ loops. Its value is a determinant of Usyukina-Davydychev ladder functions $\Phi^{(p)}$, and therefore a single-valued polylogarithm of weight $2MN$. The family is symmetric under $M \leftrightarrow N$; the slice $B_{M,1}$ reduces to the $M$-rung box ladder. In two dimensions with unit propagator weights, the same graph computes the quantum volume of a Calabi-Yau $MN$-fold.

definingIntegral.note: In dual coordinates $x_i$ (conformal $M\times N$ fishnet four-point function): $x_{ij}^2=(x_i-x_j)^2$, the four external operators sit at $x_1,\dots,x_4$, all internal lines massless, $\Phi_{M,N}$ the conformal block. Transcribed from Basso-Dixon arXiv:1705.03545 Eqs. (1)-(2); $\Phi_{M,N}$ is the determinant of ladders $\Phi^{(L)}$ per Eqs. (5)-(6).

closedForm.normalization: where $x = \frac{p_1^2 p_3^2}{s\,t}$, $y = \frac{p_2^2 p_4^2}{s\,t}$, $s = (p_1{+}p_2)^2$, $t = (p_2{+}p_3)^2$, equivalently $x = z\bar{z}$, $y = (1{-}z)(1{-}\bar{z})$. The $(M,1)$ slice reduces to the 4-mass ladder: $I_{M,1} = I_{\text{box}}^{(M)}$. Same measure convention.

basso-dixon.normalization: Basso-Dixon convention (1705.03545 Eqs. 1-6); the seeded valueExact is the CONVENTION-FREE conformal determinant BassoDixonI[M,N,z,zb] $= \det[c_{ij}\,\mathrm{PhiLadder}^{(M-N-1+i+j)}]$ over the validated Davydychev ladders at the pilot SVPL point $z=1/3$, $\bar z=1/5$ (Eq. (3) strips BD's antisymmetric prefactor; derivation log notes/families_core_batch/bd_normalization.md). $(M,1)$ members reduce EXACTLY to PhiLadder[M] (the ladder4pt value convention on the shared entries). Momentum dictionary: derived and validated in plan Task 11 before any Results merge; the $(M,1)$ chain must reduce to the validated box dictionary $+\Phi^{(M)}(u,v)/(s\,t^M)$ with no refit

refs.description: Introduces the Basso-Dixon fishnet correlator as a determinant of Usyukina-Davydychev ladder integrals $\Phi^{(L)}$, conjecturing and verifying the representation at all loop orders using integrability and Steinmann relations.
Proves the Basso-Dixon determinant formula in two-dimensional fishnet CFT from first principles, using separation of variables for the integrable $\mathrm{SL}(2,\mathbb{C})$ spin chain.
Extends the separation-of-variables approach to four-dimensional $\chi$CFT$_4$ fishnet correlators on square and honeycomb lattices, reproducing the Basso-Dixon conjecture for the scalar-only sector.

Shows that the Calabi-Yau varieties attached to two-dimensional fishnet integrals have Picard-Fuchs operators that are exterior powers of the ladder operators, recovering the Basso-Dixon formula from Calabi-Yau periods.

Identifies the $\ell$-loop Yangian-invariant fishnet integral in two dimensions as the quantum volume of the mirror Calabi-Yau $\ell$-fold, with instanton corrections appearing from $\ell = 3$ onward.

pentaladder.definition: The double pentaladder $\Omega^{(L)}$ consists of two pentagon end-caps joined by $L{-}2$ box cells, for a total of $L$ loops, six massless external legs in hexagon kinematics, and massless internal lines throughout. Its value is a pure multiple polylogarithm at every loop order, and the all-loop generating function is known in closed form via a Mellin-space finite-coupling representation. The single-pentagon variant (the penta-box ladder with five dual points) is a distinct family.

definingIntegral.note: In dual coordinates $x_i$: the two-loop double pentaladder, two pentagon caps sharing the rung $x_{rs}^2$; the numerator $x_{ar}^2 x_{bs}^2$ with $x_a,x_b$ the null-separated points ($x_{a1}^2{=}\cdots{=}x_{a4}^2{=}0$, $x_{b4}^2{=}\cdots{=}x_{b1}^2{=}0$) makes it dual-conformal and IR-finite. The $L$-loop $\Omega^{(L)}$ inserts $L{-}2$ box cells between the caps. Transcribed from Caron-Huot-Dixon et al. arXiv:1806.01361 Eq. (2.4) (numerator structure Eqs. (2.5)-(2.10)).

pentaladder.seedCaveat: Scalar skeleton only: the defining integrals $\Omega^{(L)}/\tilde\Omega^{(L)}$ carry a mandatory dual-conformal numerator (arXiv:1806.01361 eqs. 2.4-2.10); the seeded topology is the propagator skeleton, not the dressed integral, and carries no value.

refs.description: Computes the double pentaladder integral to all loop orders via a finite-coupling Mellin integral over hypergeometric functions, providing the prototype for the Steinmann hexagon function space.
Studies the cluster-algebra structure of all-loop box-ladder, penta-box-ladder, and double-penta-ladder integrals, characterizing their symbol alphabets.

elliptic-ladder.definition: The elliptic ladder $I_{L,R}$ is an $(L{+}R)$-cell ladder with two distinguished massless legs attached at a common central rung, $L$ cells to one side and $R$ to the other, together with four off-shell corner legs. It has $L{+}R$ loops, is symmetric under $L \leftrightarrow R$, and evaluates to elliptic multiple polylogarithms on a single fixed elliptic curve at all loop orders. The simplest member $I_{1,1}$ is the elliptic double box.
elliptic-ladder.functionClass: elliptic multiple polylogarithms on a single fixed elliptic curve, all $L$

definingIntegral.note: In dual coordinates $x_i$ (Family 1 of the paper): the $(L{+}R)$-cell ladder chain over loop points $x_{l_i}$, with $x_{ij}^2=(x_i-x_j)^2$; the two distinguished legs $p_5,p_{10}$ attach at the central rung ($R$ cells left of $x_5$, $L$ cells right of $x_{10}$), the numerator restores dual conformality, $I_{L,R}=I_{R,L}$. Transcribed from McLeod et al. arXiv:2301.07965 Eq. (9).

refs.description: Identifies two infinite families of ten-point Feynman diagrams generalizing the elliptic double box, proving they evaluate to elliptic multiple polylogarithms on a single fixed elliptic curve at all loop orders.

Revisits maximal-codimension residues of traintrack and elliptic-ladder integrals via Cauchy and Schubert analysis in both Feynman-parameter and momentum-twistor space, identifying the Calabi-Yau geometry at each loop order.

traintrack.definition: The traintrack integral is a planar chain of $L$ box cells with massless internal lines, four off-shell corner legs, and $2L{-}2$ massless legs along the rails. At $L$ loops it evaluates to a weight-$(L{+}1)$ hyperlogarithm whose periods live on a Calabi-Yau $(L{-}1)$-fold: the $L = 1$ member is the four-mass box, $L = 2$ is elliptic, $L = 3$ involves a K3 surface, and $L = 4$ a Calabi-Yau threefold.

definingIntegral.note: In dual coordinates: the planar $L$-box chain over loop points $\ell_1,\dots,\ell_L$ ($\ell_{L+1}\equiv b_0$), with $(a,b)\equiv(x_a-x_b)^2$ the squared dual separation; massless internal lines, off-shell legs $a_j,b_j$, and a dual-conformal numerator. Transcribed from Bourjaily et al. arXiv:1805.09326 Eq. (4).

refs.description: Introduces the traintrack integral family and shows it involves weight-$(L{+}1)$ hyperlogarithms over Calabi-Yau $(L{-}1)$-folds at $L$ loops, identifying the K3 surface at three loops and a Calabi-Yau threefold at four loops.
Embeds the Calabi-Yau geometries arising from traintrack integrals as degree-$2k$ hypersurfaces in $k$-dimensional weighted projective space, providing a uniform geometric framework for the family.

Describes the leading-singularity locus of the traintrack family directly in momentum-twistor space, recovering the elliptic curve at two loops and the K3 surface at three loops from twistor geometry.

Extends the traintrack construction by sequentially intersecting traintrack diagrams, showing the resulting integrals involve $(L{-}1)$-dimensional Calabi-Yau varieties and possess a nested structure of second-order differential operators.

tardigrade.definition: The tardigrade is a nonplanar graph in the Calabi-Yau bestiary, defined at even loop order $L$ with $2(L{+}1)$ massless propagators, five external legs, and marginal power counting in $D = 4$. Its geometry saturates the maximal rigidity bound $2(L{-}1)$, verified through eleven loops. The two-loop member is a K3 surface of Picard rank 11 and has been studied with massive internal lines as a separate kinematic configuration.

definingIntegral.note: Massless marginal $\phi^4$ integral in the Symanzik parametric form: projective integral over $\mathbb{P}^{2L+1}$ ($E=2(L+1)$ Feynman parameters $x_i$), $\mathcal{F}$ the second Symanzik polynomial of the tardigrade graph (linear in every $x_i$ in the all-massless case). Transcribed from Bourjaily-McLeod-von Hippel-Wilhelm arXiv:1810.07689 Eq. (5) (the bestiary integral; $\mathcal{F}$ defined in Eq. (2)).

refs.description: Introduces the tardigrade as a nonplanar graph whose Feynman integral involves Calabi-Yau geometry at each even loop order, establishing the rigidity bound $2(L{-}1)$ and verifying saturation through eleven loops.
Determines the Hodge structure of the two-loop tardigrade graph hypersurface, identifying its motive as that of a K3 surface with Picard number 11.

paramecium.definition: The paramecium is a nonplanar graph in the Calabi-Yau bestiary, defined at odd loop order $L$ with $2(L{+}1)$ massless propagators, six external legs (two off-shell and four massless), and marginal power counting in $D = 4$. It saturates the maximal rigidity bound $2(L{-}1)$ and is the odd-loop companion to the tardigrade. Nontrivial members begin at $L = 3$; the $L = 1$ case degenerates to the four-mass box.

definingIntegral.note: Massless marginal $\phi^4$ integral in the Symanzik parametric form: projective integral over $\mathbb{P}^{2L+1}$ ($E=2(L+1)$ Feynman parameters $x_i$), $\mathcal{F}$ the second Symanzik polynomial of the paramecium graph (linear in every $x_i$ in the all-massless case). Transcribed from Bourjaily-McLeod-von Hippel-Wilhelm arXiv:1810.07689 Eq. (5) ($\mathcal{F}$ defined in Eq. (2)).

refs.description: Introduces the paramecium as the odd-loop companion to the tardigrade in the Calabi-Yau bestiary, demonstrating maximal rigidity $2(L{-}1)$ at each odd loop order from six external legs.

amoeba.definition: The amoeba is a nonplanar graph in the Calabi-Yau bestiary, defined at odd loop order $L$ with $2(L{+}1)$ massless propagators and four off-shell external legs. It is marginal in $D = 4$; the three-loop member is the complete bipartite graph $K_{2,4}$ and has non-maximal rigidity, while the maximal bound $2(L{-}1)$ is saturated from $L = 5$ onward.

definingIntegral.note: Massless marginal $\phi^4$ integral in the Symanzik parametric form: projective integral over $\mathbb{P}^{2L+1}$ ($E=2(L+1)$ Feynman parameters $x_i$), $\mathcal{F}$ the second Symanzik polynomial of the amoeba graph (linear in every $x_i$ in the all-massless case). Transcribed from Bourjaily-McLeod-von Hippel-Wilhelm arXiv:1810.07689 Eq. (5) ($\mathcal{F}$ defined in Eq. (2)).

refs.description: Introduces the amoeba as a nonplanar four-point graph in the Calabi-Yau bestiary with non-maximal rigidity at three loops ($K_{2,4}$) and saturation of the bound $2(L{-}1)$ from five loops onward.

ice-cone.definition: The ice-cream cone is a banana graph capped by a triangle: $L$ loops, $L{+}2$ propagators all carrying equal internal mass $m$, with two massless external legs at the banana endpoints and one off-shell leg at the apex carrying momentum $q$. It depends on the single dimensionless ratio $q^2/m^2$. In two dimensions it shares the same Calabi-Yau $(L{-}1)$-fold periods as the banana (two copies), and an all-loop master integral basis is known. The $L = 1$ member is the equal-mass triangle.

definingIntegral.note: Momentum-space three-point ice cone, $L$ loops: $L$ equal-mass banana propagators $k_a^2-m^2$ plus the two triangle propagators carrying massless $p_1,p_2$ ($p_1^2{=}p_2^2{=}0$), off-shell apex $q{=}p_1{+}p_2$, $s{=}q^2$; depends on $s/m^2$. Transcribed from Duhr-Klemm-Nega-Tancredi arXiv:2212.09550 Eqs. (1) and (6).

refs.description: Obtains fully analytic results for the equal-mass ice cone at all loop orders in two dimensions, showing its maximal cuts decompose into two copies of the banana Calabi-Yau periods, and solves the differential equations in terms of iterated integrals.

banana.definition: The banana (sunrise) graph consists of two vertices joined by $L{+}1$ edges, giving $L$ loops with equal internal masses and a single kinematic scale $t = p^2/m^2$. Its Feynman integral evaluates to periods of a Calabi-Yau $(L{-}1)$-fold; the differential equation is $\epsilon$-factorized at all loop orders, and the all-$L$ structure is governed by a GKZ system.

definingIntegral.note: Equal-mass $L$-loop banana (sunset) in the maximal-cut / Symanzik parametric form: $L{+}1$ Feynman parameters $x_i$ (one per equal-mass propagator joining the two vertices), $\mu_L$ the projective measure on the simplex $\sigma_L$, single scale $t=p^2/m^2$. Transcribed from Bonisch-Fischbach-Klemm-Nega-Safari arXiv:2008.10574 Eq. (1) (equal-mass case $\xi_i{=}1$).

refs.description: Solves the $L$-loop banana amplitude with general masses via the GKZ system of the Barth-Nieto Calabi-Yau $(L{-}1)$-fold, constructing a complete basis of periods and computing explicit mass dependences through three loops.

Determines the complete analytic structure of the all-loop banana amplitude with arbitrary masses, computing the leading logarithmic behavior from a novel $\Gamma$-class evaluation and the imaginary part from the mirror Calabi-Yau.

Casts the differential equation for the $L$-loop equal-mass banana into $\epsilon$-factorized form at all loop orders, exploiting the self-duality of the underlying Calabi-Yau operator to obtain systematic $\epsilon$-expansions.

fishnet-multileg-2d.definition: The multi-leg fishnet is the triangle-track sequence $Z_L$ on the trivalent (hexagonal) tiling in $D = 2$: a strip of $L$ triangles with $L$ loops and $L{+}2$ off-shell external legs, where every propagator carries weight $2/3$. The simplest members $Z_2$ and $Z_3$ reduce to the box and pentagon by the star-triangle identity, while at higher $L$ the geometry involves Calabi-Yau periods; the lowest nontrivial case is a genus-two Picard curve.

definingIntegral.note: In dual (position) coordinates $\xi_i$ on the trivalent (hexagonal) tiling: every line carries power $D/V=2/3$ in $D{=}2$ (valency $V{=}3$); $\alpha_j$ the external points, $\bar\alpha$ their collection. The seeded representative is the triangle-track $Z_L$. Transcribed from Duhr-Klemm-Loebbert-Nega-Porkert arXiv:2402.19034 Eq. (2.1).

fishnet-multileg-2d.functionClass: multi-leg Calabi-Yau periods (Picard curve at lowest order)

refs.description: Generalizes the geometric analysis of square fishnet integrals to the hexagonal (three-point) tiling in two dimensions, identifying the simplest members as Picard curves and higher-loop members as Calabi-Yau varieties fixed by Yangian and permutation symmetries.

generalised-ladder.definition: The generalised ladder is a three-point integral indexed by the loop order $L$ and a binary word $m \in \{0,1\}^{L-1}$, giving $2^{L-1}$ topologically distinct members at each $L$. The momentum-space graph is the fan $F_L$: a hub vertex joined to every vertex of an $L$-edge path, with three off-shell external legs. Its value is a single-valued polylogarithm, and the all-zeros word evaluates to the same $\Phi^{(L)}$ as the four-point box ladder via the conformal three-to-four-point completion. The two-point vacuum limits of this family recover the zigzags and wheels.
generalised-ladder.functionClass: single-valued polylogarithms (superset of ladders, wheels, zigzags)

definingIntegral.note: In dual coordinates $x_i$ (Drummond's generalised three-point ladder): spine vertices $x_{b_1}\dots x_{b_L}$, top attaches to $x_2$, bottom to both $x_0,x_1$, each intermediate $x_{b_i}$ to $x_{a_i}$ ($a_i\in\{0,1\}$), $x_{ij}^2=(x_i-x_j)^2$; indexed by loop order $L$ and binary word $m=a_1\cdots a_{L-1}$ ($2^{L-1}$ members, seeded at the all-zeros word). Transcribed from Drummond arXiv:1207.3824 Eq. (2.7).

generalised-ladder.normalization: Fan $F_L$: $G^{(L)}(p_1^2,p_2^2,p_3^2) = \Phi^{(L)}(x,y)/(p_3^2)^L$ with $x = p_1^2/p_3^2$, $y = p_2^2/p_3^2$; evaluates to the same $\Phi^{(L)}$ as the 4-mass ladder

closedForm.normalization: where $\Phi^{(L)}$ is the same function as in the 4-mass ladder. Three off-shell external momenta $p_1, p_2, p_3$ with $p_1 + p_2 + p_3 = 0$. Same measure convention.

refs.description: Introduces and solves an infinite class of three-point loop integrals parametrized by a binary word, expressing them in terms of single-valued polylogarithms and relating the vacuum limits to zigzag and wheel periods.

triangle-ladder.definition: The Usyukina-Davydychev triangle ladder $T^{(L)}$ consists of $(L{-}1)$ box cells capped by a triangle at the apex, with two rails of $L$ edges converging to a shared apex vertex and $L$ rungs connecting the rails at each level. It has $2L{+}1$ vertices, $3L$ massless internal propagators, $L$ loops, and three off-shell external legs at the two base vertices and the apex. The triangle ladder evaluates to the same $\Phi^{(L)}$ as the four-point box ladder and the generalised-ladder fan via the conformal magic identity, but is a genuinely different topology from both for $L \ge 2$: the triangle ladder has $V = 2L{+}1$, $E = 3L$ versus $V = L{+}2$, $E = 2L{+}1$ for the fan. Only $L = 1$ (the triangle) is shared.

definingIntegral.note: Momentum-space $L$-loop triangle ladder: two rails of propagators converging to the apex vertex carrying $p_3$, $L$ rungs, all $3L$ internal lines massless, three off-shell external legs $p_1^2,p_2^2,p_3^2$ with $p_1{+}p_2{+}p_3{=}0$. Transcribed from Usyukina-Davydychev, PLB 298 (1993) 363 and PLB 305 (1993) 136.

closedForm.normalization: where $\Phi^{(L)}$ is the same function as in the 4-mass ladder. Three off-shell external momenta $p_1, p_2, p_3$ with $p_1 + p_2 + p_3 = 0$. Same measure convention.

triangle-ladder.normalization: $T^{(L)}(p_1^2,p_2^2,p_3^2) = \Phi^{(L)}(x,y)/(p_3^2)^L$ with $x=p_1^2/p_3^2$, $y=p_2^2/p_3^2$; same $\Phi^{(L)}$ as the 4-mass ladder and the generalised ladder fan

refs.description: Introduces the method for evaluating massless three-point ladder integrals at arbitrary loop order, establishing the triangle-ladder family.
Derives the exact closed-form result $\Phi^{(L)}(x,y)$ for the $L$-loop triangle and box ladder integrals using the uniqueness method.
Defines the generalised three-point ladders indexed by binary words and shows they evaluate to single-valued polylogarithms; the all-zeros word reduces to the triangle ladder.

ngon.definition: The one-loop $n$-gon (or $A_n$ polygon) has $n$ massless internal propagators arranged in a cycle with $n$ massless external legs. In $D = n$ dimensions the integral is finite and evaluates to the volume of a hyperbolic $(n{-}1)$-simplex (Davydychev-Delbourgo 1997). For $n = 3$ (triangle) the result involves Clausen functions; for $n = 4$ (box) it is the Murakami-Yano formula in 16 dilogarithms. The family includes the bubble ($n = 2$), triangle, box, pentagon, hexagon, heptagon, and octagon.

closedForm.normalization: $\mathrm{Vol}_{n-1}(S)$ is the volume of the ideal hyperbolic $(n{-}1)$-simplex determined by the $n \times n$ kinematic matrix $S$ (with $S_{ii} = 0$ for massless external legs). Computed via the Schläfli formula: $d\,\mathrm{Vol}_{n-1} = -\tfrac{1}{n{-}2}\sum_{i<j} \mathrm{Vol}_{n-3}(\mathrm{face}_{ij})\,d\theta_{ij}$, where $\theta_{ij}$ are dihedral angles. For even $n$: weight $n/2$ in polylogarithms (Li$_2$ at $n = 4$, Li$_3$ at $n = 6$). For odd $n$: reduces to even-dimensional sub-volumes. Measure: $I_n = e^{\varepsilon\gamma_E}\mu^{2\varepsilon}\int d^n k/(i\pi^{n/2})\prod_e(-q_e^2)^{-1}$ at $\varepsilon = 0$.

refs.description: Establishes that the one-loop $n$-point integral in $D = n$ dimensions equals the volume of a geodesic simplex in hyperbolic space, providing the geometric interpretation that underlies all subsequent work on this family.

Derives explicit closed-form evaluations of all-mass $n$-gon integrals in $n$ dimensions for $n = 2, 3, 4, 5$ using Schläfli-type volume formulas, and establishes the symbol structure for all $n$ via orthoscheme decomposition.
Gives the cleanest modern presentation of one-loop integrals as volumes of orthoschemes, with explicit evaluation of the massless hexagon in $D = 6$ via the Kellerhals formula.

Derives dimensionally-regulated one-loop integrals and the reduction of the $n$-point scalar integral to $(n{-}1)$-point integrals in $D = 4 - 2\varepsilon$, establishing the standard framework for one-loop amplitude computations.
Comprehensive textbook on Feynman integrals; Appendix on scalar one-loop integrals lists all massless box mass configurations with explicit dilogarithmic formulas in $D = 4 - 2\varepsilon$.
